sentences.text: Yes, the-- in-- in Europe, it was Moshe.
That's the name from my parents and grandparents.
No.
Moshe, or sometimes, they called me [NON-ENGLISH].
Because I had two names, Moshe [NON-ENGLISH] Pantirer.
I was 70 in June.
In Krakow, Poland.
In the town of Krakow.
Was called the Jewish quarters.
We lived on [NON-ENGLISH] 16 near the [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH] all the Jewish people live.
Kazmielz.
K-A-Z-M-I-E-L-Z.
Lazar Pinchas.
My mother's name was Bella Sarah.
Lazar Pinchas.
Bella Sarah.
I had, ah, another brother who was born in 1923.
And then I was the second son.
Then I had two more brothers.
One was born in 1927.
His name was Chaim Yosef.
My older brothers was Alte Hirsch.
And the fourth son, my third brother, was Mordechai Yehuda.
And then in '31, my sister was born, Esther [NON-ENGLISH].
In 1933, another brother was born.
His name was Yisrael David Tevye.
In 1935, my youngest sister, second sister, was named Chaya Rechel.
No.
No, I did not have no grandparents when-- I remember.
I only had, uh, a grandmother who lived by-- by an uncle of mine, by my father's brother, Avrum Pantirer in Ozarow.
Ozarow.
O-Z-O-R-W.
Ozarow.
I had only one uncle who lived not far from us.
Isaac Levenstein, my mother's brother.
And his wife was Sarah Levenstein.
And they had two children.
One was born in 1933.
And one was born in 1935.
We lived not far from the Talmud Torah.
It's a Hebrew school.
And normal, my days would be early in the morning I will get up, help my father to distribute some milk and eggs.
He had a little store.
Then we will go to the synagogue to pray.
And by-- we had breakfast. 9 o'clock in the morning, we will start school, Hebrew school in Talmud Torah.
I was there from 9 to 12.
From 12 to 1, we went home to have, uh, our major meal.
It's like dinner.
Then from 1 to 4, we took Polish, history, arithmetic, the regular schools.
And at 4 o'clock, we only had time to grab a piece of chocolate or coffee or chocolate milk, and we went again to the same Hebrew school as we were in the morning.
And from there we were from 4 to 6. 6 o'clock, I would normally get sometimes $0.05, five groszy or 10 groszy.
It all depends what I would get.
We would stop by in the milk store, and we would buy a sandwich.
For $0.05 I used to get a roll and butter.
And if I had $0.10, I used to get a piece of herring too.
And then with that, we will go to a synagogue on [NON-ENGLISH] Street, that's the Belzer synagogue.
And over there, we will pray and we will do our homework.
Because at home it was very hard to do homework.
First, the lighting was not good.
So in that synagogue, we will-- we had some elderly people who took a liking to our studying.
That they will sometimes buy a chocolate or some candies that they will study with us.
I was very religious.
And I was 14 years old when the war broke out.
And I strongly believe that if one day I should not put on my tefillin, that either my hand will fall off or something terrible will happen to my parents.
That's how strict religious I was.
Were both sides of your family religious, both your parents?
Did they come from Orthodox homes?
Yes.
A hat.
We always wore a hat.
There was no such thing as going without your, uh, hat.
We wore locks, uh, by our ears.
And we were dressed-- I was still young, but I was dressed, uh, modest, not flashy clothes, um, mostly grey, dark grey, or black.
Earlocks.
Yes, my father had a beard, and--
Yes.
In-- in the h-- in the house there was a special place, in the building.
And from over there, customers will come in.
Mostly we will carry it to the houses.
We'll distribute them to the houses.
That's what we do in the morning.
Yes, my older brother.
And the other ones were still too young.
And my mother helped out.
No, An apartment.
It was about 100 tenants.
[COUGHS]
It was cobblestones.
It was a-- a street full of buildings, uh, mostly 100 tenants.
Some of them were a little smaller.
Some were a little larger.
And in the street, every second or third building had a little synagogue.
In the street where we lived mostly lived Jewish people, except the-- the super was Gentile.
And, of course, at 10 o'clock at night, he will lock the door.
But in our building there was a courtyard.
And the courtyard we will be play in.
By foot.
It wasn't too far away.
Mostly we did everything by foot.
There was no cars.
There were some people, maybe a few families in Krakow.
But that was already in further-- more richer sections.
No, We didn't.
Yes.
I did-- really did not come across non-Jewish people.
Aye.
There was not-- there was not organizations like you have it here in America.
You belong to a shul, a synagogue, and to a rebbe.
Our-- most of our time was spent being busy with-- you can imagine my mother had seven children.
I will never forget that the 1937 she had children every second year.
In 1937, she had no children.
So she said, God doesn't want me to have any more children.
And she did the cooking and the washing and the ironing.
And-- and we made plenty holes in the-- ripped our shirts and ripped our pants.
And she did all that work.
So she had no time for any other things.
My father, after he finished the work, he would-- loved the synagogue.
And he mostly davened.
And [NON-ENGLISH] 22 there was the rebbe from [NON-ENGLISH].
It was called the [NON-ENGLISH] Rebbe.
And he was a composer.
He composed a lot of new songs.
And my father would know how to sing.
He was not exactly a cantor, but like a-- a [INAUDIBLE].
And he would be-- when he-- the rebbe composed a new song, a new prayer, he would go over with his son, [NON-ENGLISH], and with my father.
So that's how he was busy.
[NON-ENGLISH] Rebbe.
Oh, yeah.
Sure.
And the Sabbath was observed.
And the street, there was not a single store open on the Sabbath.
No, there was no problem.
Well, in '37 and '38, refugees from Germany, Jewish people, were chased out and they would come to Krakow.
And they will talk about the-- certain things that happened in Germany.
But there was always an excuse that the Germans chasing out the Jews who came from Poland, who were not born in Germany.
That they took away jobs from the Germans or they cheat on the Germans.
And all our politicking was done in the shul.
Because, uh, we had no radios.
Like having a radio would be like in [INAUDIBLE] Park to have a television.
We had no radios.
We rely on newspapers.
There are a lot of Jewish papers that talk-- Mostly, like I said, in the shul there's a prayer in the afternoon called mincha.
And then there's an evening prayer that's going maariv.
Between mincha and maariv, mincha you must do before sunset, and maariv you must do when it's dark.
So there was always a half hour, 40 minutes in between.
Most people will sit at the table and learn Talmud.
But when-- the politicking of the world-- that the Anschluss, Hitler with the Austria then taking over Czechoslovakia.
As a kid, you didn't dare speak out.
But I listened to the discussion of the younger people.
Was always alert.
The younger people, I'm not talking about myself, a 14-year-old.
But the ones 20, 25 30 years old will say, we've got to do something.
Hitler wrote
And the other people will say, what do you know about the Germans?
You're [NON-ENGLISH].
Your mother's milk is still on your lips.
We know the Germans.
We were in Krakow in WWI.
We know the Germans, and the Germans will never do this thing.
And finally they said, he's doing it already.
He's throwing people out from-- there was a Kristallnacht.
And they was burning shuls.
They were throwing people out from the country.
They take away their rights.
So the discussion went-- the Ger-- Hitler is crazy.
Hitler may want to do it.
But the German people will never allow it.
And finally it got to the talk, ah, he's doing it.
So the other people will always say, why would they do something to us?
What did we do to the Germans?
We are law abiding citizens.
And it was-- the commotion was that the whole shul could hear, the argument back and forth.
And finally, the elderly people won the-- the argument, because they said, we have a big God, ein grosser Gott.
And God will help us.
The believing in God was so strong that we felt we are protected by God.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
Yiddish and-- and Polish.
Of course, there was-- there was no question about it.
It was written up, uh, in Europe some terrible things.
But again, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] nobody was prepared.
First, they said, he's not going to touch Poland.
If he's going to touch Poland, France and England and America, they are going to declare war against the Germans.
And Hitler will not be that crazy to start fighting with the whole world.
So there was, ah-- I see it now, it was wishful thinking.
But on the other hand, there was no other choice.
And a family like our family, we had no place to go.
You needed a lot of money.
To Palestine, they wouldn't let you in with seven children.
And you needed a lot of money to buy those cards, just the trip alone.
And to America we couldn't go, because there was a very small quota.
We came under the Polish quota.
And the Polish quota was very small.
And again, the trip alone for us it was impossible.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Well, it was before the war, and nobody wanted to undertake communism.
First of all, we didn't want to be denied our religious right.
Who wanted to have their children grow up and be a communist?
We did not-- the feeling of the Nazi Germans yet.
But we knew how bad the communists are.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]
No, we-- we-- we did not have room.
But they stayed, uh, in our neighbor's place.
And they stayed in public, ah, Jewish public housing, like in schools [INAUDIBLE].
We went into a guy who was making materials-- who had some materials pieces, ah, from clothing, a tailor.
And he gave us the pieces.
And we make it like a baseball, a ball.
And we-- we kicked that ball around.
We played mostly soccer.
I remember just a few months before the war, we already chipped in-- everybody chipped in $0.10.
And we had a regular soccer ball.
Our major sport was playing soccer.
Our spare time was to study.
We didn't know about going to a movie, because it wasn't proper.
And we didn't have the money to go to a movie.
But we were very happy.
We played with-- played chess, dominoes.
We did not have computers, but-- other toys.
But, uh-- there was always something to do.
Yes.
The-- the-- one summer, in Poland there was a law that if a grownup had a ticket to go to a place during the summer months when there was vacation, he could take a-- a child with him.
So I would go to the train, just one summer, and somebody took me along to see my grandmother in Ozarow.
That was the only time that I remember being on vacation.
It was by train about six, seven hours.
No.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We barely made out.
We were comfortable.
There was a Shabbat.
There was fish and there was meat.
And there was, uh, dessert.
And for the holidays, we used to get a new outfit.
But that's about it, nothing extravagant.
But we had a good life.
The family was together.
We ate-- even during the weekdays when we ate dinner, 12 o'clock, or Shabbat, nobody would dare stand up from the table before my father finished his [INAUDIBLE] There was respect.
Yiddish and balbatish.
Dinner was during lunch time.
12 and 1 o'clock.
In the evening in the regular days, the weekdays, we would go to a shul to the stiebl and do my homework, rehash the Talmud that I learned during that day.
[NON-ENGLISH] stiebl.
